highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.149 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds a per-category breakdown to the `/usage` command so you can see exactly what's consuming your limits — skills, subagents, plugins, or MCP servers. The `/diff` detail view now supports keyboard scrolling with arrow keys, j/k, Page Up/Down, and more. Markdown output also renders properly, making Claude Code's interface more readable.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.147 Released — /code-review Command & Pinned Sessions
highlights.summary: The `/simplify` command has been renamed to `/code-review` and now reports correctness bugs in addition to style suggestions. Pinned background sessions (created with `Ctrl+T` in `claude agents`) now persist when idle and automatically restart to apply updates, making long-running agent workflows much more reliable.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has announced Claude Opus 4.7, the latest model in the Opus family. This is a significant model update that Claude Code users will want to be aware of, as new models typically bring improved coding capabilities and reasoning. Check the Anthropic blog for details on how to use it within your Claude Code setup.

highlights.title: Show HN: Spec-Driven Development Workflow for Claude Code
highlights.summary: This workflow proposes using structured specification documents to guide Claude Code through complex tasks by first generating requirements, then a design, and finally splitting implementation into subtasks. The two-dimensional decomposition approach — specs first, then parallel subtasks — helps avoid context drift and produces more consistent results. This is a great pattern for developers tackling large features with agentic coding tools.

highlights.title: Microsoft Starts Canceling Claude Code Licenses
highlights.summary: Reports indicate Microsoft is discontinuing Claude Code licenses for some users, signaling a potential strategic shift. Developers relying on employer-provided Claude Code access via Microsoft should verify their license status and explore direct Anthropic subscriptions as an alternative. This is worth monitoring as it may affect teams using Claude Code through enterprise Microsoft agreements.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial offers a concise but comprehensive walkthrough of Claude Code's core features, aimed at developers who want to get productive quickly without sitting through a multi-hour course. It covers key commands, workflows, and practical use cases in just 30 minutes. A great starting point if you're new to Claude Code or want a fast refresher.

highlights.title: Claude Code Tutorial: Beginner to Advanced in 20 Minutes
highlights.summary: This fast-paced video covers Claude Code from the basics all the way to advanced usage patterns in just 20 minutes, making it ideal for developers who learn best by watching. Topics likely include setting up Claude Code, running commands, and leveraging agentic features for real projects. It's an efficient way to level up your Claude Code skills in a single sitting.

highlights.title: Session Amnesia: The Hidden Cost of Stateless AI Coding Assistants
highlights.summary: This article explores how stateless AI coding tools lose context between sessions, forcing developers to re-explain their project structure and decisions repeatedly — a problem the author calls 'session amnesia'. The piece is a useful primer for understanding why persistent context (via CLAUDE.md files or pinned sessions) matters so much for productive AI-assisted development. Claude Code users can mitigate this by maintaining a well-structured `CLAUDE.md` at the project root.

tips_of_the_day: Use `/usage` in Claude Code to get a per-category breakdown of your limits — if MCP servers are consuming most of your quota, consider consolidating to only the tools you actively need for your current project.

Keep a `CLAUDE.md` file at your project root with a brief summary of your architecture, key conventions, and recent decisions. This acts as persistent memory across sessions and dramatically reduces the time Claude spends re-learning your codebase.

When tackling a large feature, try spec-driven prompting: ask Claude Code to first write a requirements doc, then a design doc, then break the work into numbered subtasks — only then start implementing. This two-step decomposition produces far more consistent results than a single open-ended prompt.
